does: This paper introduces EvoSynth, a multi-agent framework that automates the discovery of jailbreak attacks against large language models by evolving executable code rather than refining prompts. Instead of searching over attack wording or strategy choices, EvoSynth optimizes attack algorithms at the code level, enabling the system to discover complex procedures with control flow, reusable logic, branching, and failure-driven repair mechanisms. The framework employs multiple agents that collaboratively engineer and evolve code-based attack algorithms, incorporating a self-correction loop that allows the system to iteratively rewrite algorithms based on target model feedback and failed attempts. Experiments demonstrate that this code-space optimization approach achieves substantially higher attack success rates than prior prompt-level methods.
connects: This work extends a line of research on automated red teaming and adversarial testing of LLMs, building on earlier frameworks that search for effective jailbreak prompts through optimization methods. Prior work has primarily focused on optimizing within prompt space—adjusting wording, structure, and strategy choices—as seen in approaches like prompt-based evolutionary algorithms and gradient-based attack optimization. EvoSynth responds to the fundamental limitation that prompts alone cannot capture complex attack procedures and algorithmic logic. The paper connects to research on program synthesis and autonomous code generation, leveraging multi-agent systems and evolutionary algorithms traditionally used for automated program discovery. It also builds on work in failure-driven learning and self-correction mechanisms that enable iterative refinement based on execution feedback.
enables: This work opens several research directions in adversarial AI safety and defense. By demonstrating that code-level search discovers more sophisticated attack procedures than prompt optimization alone, it suggests that future red teaming frameworks should operate at higher levels of abstraction beyond natural language. The code-synthesis approach enables discovery of novel attack classes that may not be easily expressible as prompts, potentially identifying vulnerabilities in LLMs that static jailbreak collections miss. This capability could accelerate the development of more robust defenses by revealing structural weaknesses in model behavior. Additionally, the self-correction loop and multi-agent methodology could inspire more general approaches to autonomous vulnerability discovery in AI systems, extending beyond LLMs to other domains requiring systematic adversarial testing and continuous adaptation.
